Hudson Estuary Resilience Effort

network_affiliation
[[BioFi Project]]
background_section
## Background Lenape tradition names part of this estuary **Mawa**—a meeting place. The Hudson is a tidal freshwater wedge where Adirondack runoff meets Atlantic salt: twice daily the current reverses. It is also a social crossroads—Appalachian uplands, farms and cities, global diplomacy at the river’s mouth. The estuary covers [5,312 square miles from upper Manhattan to Troy](https://youtu.be/YPJ2Thbgbdc)—HERE’s home ground for shared bioregional work. Most of the basin lies in New York, with extensions into Connecticut, New Jersey, and Massachusetts. It holds [roughly 200 fish species, 19 rare-bird types, 140 rare plants, and 11 significant biodiversity areas](https://youtu.be/YPJ2Thbgbdc)—and figures in the early U.S. conservation story. The Appalachian Trail’s 2,000-mile route crosses the region, drawing long-distance hikers each year. Pressures include pollution affecting [drinking water for more than 100,000 people](https://youtu.be/YPJ2Thbgbdc), combined sewer overflows, flood and sea-level risk, housing stress, invasives, and—less visible to many—rising forest-fire exposure in the Northeast. ### Origins and Network The Hudson Estuary Resilience Effort (HERE) grew out of **Partners for Climate Action Hudson Valley**. For roughly five years, that collaboration concentrated grants on four interlinked domains—ecology, food, culture, and shelter—testing how place-based funding could track cross-cutting resilience in the estuary. HERE carries that guild-shaped logic forward as its own organising frame.

temp
improper location but good for now?
description
Supports regenerative transition through a guild-based approach focused on food, shelter, ecology, and culture, working across the Hudson River estuary region to foster local collaboration, fund place-based solutions, and steward long-term bioregional resilience.
vision_legacy_section
## Vision and Legacy HERE treats the tidal Hudson as both ecology and social infrastructure: guilds, fund, and narrative media as ways to align regeneration with the communities who live here. The aim is organising and capital that stay near the problem—**here**, in the estuary’s “meeting place.” A timeline ties guild development, fund-building, and congress to a single transition strategy: local action linked to regional-scale resilience.
website
https://www.herehudsonvalley.org/
bioregional_context
The Hudson River Estuary stretches 5,312 square miles from New York City to Troy, a tidal freshwater region hosting rare biodiversity, vibrant communities, and cultural history, but facing threats from pollution, housing crises, and climate impacts like flooding and sea level rise.
category
Bioregional Organizer
